blocks.content: She smelled like something warm — some combination of her shampoo and the night air from outside and whatever she'd put on before they came. He'd noticed it at the door and was noticing it now and was doing his best not to make a thing of noticing it.

They didn't talk. The music was enough to make silence comfortable and the floor was full enough that they were pressed a little closer than the formal distance you maintained with someone who was just your friend who you were just dancing with at homecoming as friends.
He felt her shift — just slightly, leaning in rather than away — and his hand at her waist tightened without him meaning it to.
The song moved. The lights stayed low. Across the room Eli had both hands up, doing something that could generously be called dancing. Kit almost laughed. He looked back down at Daisy.
She was already looking at him.

Not the sideways look she used when she was teasing him, not the reading look she used when she was figuring him out. Just — looking at him. Directly, without the usual cover. And the noise and the music and the orange ribbon on the basketball hoops and the hundred and fifty other people in the gym all went a little distant, and Kit thought: oh.
Oh, this is what it feels like when you stop pretending.
